coredata.dc:title: Red-emitting carbon dots as fluorescent probes for the rapid detection of brilliant blue FCF in foods
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Qi
Duan, Xin-Jiao
Lv, Hai-Tao
Wang, Li-Tao
coredata.dc:description: To avoid the interference of blue background fluorescence from food substrates, red-emitting carbon dots (R-CDs) were designed for the detection of brilliant blue FCF (BB). The synthesized R-CDs exhibited good photoluminescence stability and emitted red fluorescence at 627 nm. BB exhibits a linear fluorescence quenching effect on R-CDs in the range of 0.2–40 μM with a limit of detection of 84.00 nM. The fluorescence quenching mechanism can be explained by the inner filter effect. Food samples showed no fluorescence signals in the red region. Common substrates also had no visible effect on the fluorescence of the R-CDs. The developed method is practical for the detection of BB in cocktails and beverages without sample pretreatment. Using R-CDs as fluorescent probes, a sensitive, selective, and accurate BB detection method was developed.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Red-emitting carbon dots
Fluorescence
Brilliant blue FCF
Quenching mechanism
Inner filter effect

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of the BB standard solution
Comparison of the proposed method with others

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (No. ZR2020QB087 ), the High-Level Talents Scientific Research Foundation of Qingdao Agricultural University (No. 6651120016 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Diagram for the preparation of R-CDs and detection mechanism.

(a) TEM image of R-CDs, the inset is the statistical distribution of particle sizes; (b) FTIR spectrum of R-CDs; (c) XPS survey of R-CDs; (d) XPS C1s spectrum of R-CDs; (e) XPS O1s spectrum of R-CDs; (f) XPS N1s spectrum of R-CDs; (g) XPS P2p spectrum of R-CDs; (h) UV–vis absorption spectrum of R-CDs; (i) Fluorescence emission spectra of R-CDs at different excitation wavelengths.

(a) Fluorescence spectra in the presence of different concentrations of BB; (b) Standard curves of Log(F0/F) versus concentrations of BB in the range of 0.2–40 μM; (c) Fluorescence quenching effect of interfering substances on R-CDs; (d) Fluorescence spectra for the detection of real samples.

(a) UV–vis absorbance spectra of BB and fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of R-CDs; (b) Fluorescence decay trace of R-CDs in the absence and presence of BB; (c) UV–vis absorbance spectra of R-CDs, BB and R-CDs + BB mixture.
Comparison of linear range and LOD of various nanomaterials-based fluorescent analysis methods for BB.

head.ce:title: Red-emitting carbon dots as fluorescent probes for the rapid detection of brilliant blue FCF in foods

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Sciences, Qingdao Agricultural University, Qingdao, 266109, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Sciences, Qingdao Agricultural University, Qingdao 266109, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Jining Medical University, Jining, 272067, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Jining Medical University, Jining 272067, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To avoid the interference of blue background fluorescence from food substrates, red-emitting carbon dots (R-CDs) were designed for the detection of brilliant blue FCF (BB). The synthesized R-CDs exhibited good photoluminescence stability and emitted red fluorescence at 627 nm. BB exhibits a linear fluorescence quenching effect on R-CDs in the range of 0.2–40 μM with a limit of detection of 84.00 nM. The fluorescence quenching mechanism can be explained by the inner filter effect. Food samples showed no fluorescence signals in the red region. Common substrates also had no visible effect on the fluorescence of the R-CDs. The developed method is practical for the detection of BB in cocktails and beverages without sample pretreatment. Using R-CDs as fluorescent probes, a sensitive, selective, and accurate BB detection method was developed.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Color additives are now recognized as important components of practically all processed foods. Brilliant blue FCF (BB), a synthetic colorant, is widely used in soft drinks, jams and jellies, candies, and other foods. However, BB and its secondary products are toxic to the human body if consumed in large quantities or over a long period of time (Amchova et al., 2015). The Codex Alimentarius Commission (CAC) and governments have clearly stipulated the allowable additive amount of BB in food. Therefore, the accurate detection of BB is of great significance to food safety. Current detection methods for BB include high-performance liquid chromatography (Mathiyalagan et al., 2019; Stachova et al., 2016), colorimetry (Wang et al., 2022; Wu et al., 2022), UV–vis spectrophotometry (Bisgin, 2018; He & Jiang, 2022), surface-enhanced Raman scattering (Chen et al., 2018; Xie et al., 2019), and electrochemical sensors (Lipskikh et al., 2020; Sierra-Rosales et al., 2019). Although these methods make great contributions to food quality monitoring, the problems of complex sample pretreatment, time consumption, expensive equipment, and the need for highly skilled personnel still exist (Okeke et al., 2022). Fluorescence detection methods are sensitive, simple, and rapid, particularly those based on fluorescence quenching or probe recovery (Han et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2020; Wu & Tong, 2019; Yang et al., 2019).

Carbon dots (CDs), a type of fluorescent nanoparticle, have low toxicity, good biocompatibility, excellent dispersity, and high photoluminescence stability. In recent years, CDs have been widely used for sensing, imaging, anti-counterfeiting, and encryption (Cui et al., 2021; Huang et al., 2019; Ma et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2022). CDs are used as fluorescent probes for the detection of color additives, such as carmine (Hu et al., 2021; Su et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2016), tartrazine (Ghereghlou et al., 2021; Li et al., 2022), and sunset yellow (Yuan et al., 2016). However, applications of CDs for the detection of BB are relatively scarce. Full-color fluorescent carbon nanoparticles were synthesized from eucalyptus twigs and used as probes for the detection of BB via a fluorescence quenching mechanism (Damera et al., 2020). The optimal detection wavelength was 380 nm in the blue region. Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots were synthesized for the detection of BB based on a static quenching mechanism (Jin et al., 2019). These nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots emitted blue fluorescence with a maximum wavelength of 450 nm, limit of detection (LOD) of 94.87 nmol/L, and a linear range of 0.63–5.05 μmol/L.

These studies are valuable for monitoring BB, but their CDs either emit blue fluorescence or have optimal detection wavelengths in the blue region, and their fluorescence can be greatly interfered with by the blue background fluorescence from complex food substrates, whereas longer-wavelength emissions are ideal for these applications (Ding et al., 2020; Hallaji et al., 2021; Li et al., 2021). Hence, in this study, red-emitting CDs (R-CDs) were synthesized and designed for BB detection. The detection mechanism is based on the fluorescence quenching effect of BB on the R-CDs owing to the inner filter effect (IFE). A diagram describing the preparation of R-CDs and the detection mechanism is shown in Fig. 1 . To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report of the detection of BB using R-CDs as fluorescent probes. The morphology, composition, and fluorescence properties of the R-CDs were characterized using transmission electron microscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, and fluorescence spectroscopy. The maximum emission wavelength of the R-CDs was 627 nm, which is in the red region. When the excitation wavelength increased from 480 nm to 580 nm, the maximum emission wavelength of the R-CDs remained at 627 nm, showing excitation-independent emission characteristics. In the presence of BB, the fluorescence intensity of the R-CD solution decreased quickly; thus, the rapid detection of BB was achieved. The practicability of the established method was tested by detecting BB in cocktails and beverages. The detection results were consistent with those obtained from high-performance liquid chromatography, which verified the reliability of this R-CD-based fluorescence detection method.

O-phenylenediamine, phosphoric acid, Brilliant blue FCF, sodium chloride, potassium iodide, calcium acetate, magnesium sulfate, ferric chloride, ferrous chloride, sodium carbonate, sodium bicarbonate, sodium sulfate, glucose, sucrose, fructose, citric acid, ascorbic acid, vitamin B1, and vitamin B6 were purchased from Macklin Biochemical Technology Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ultrapure water was obtained from a Merck Millipore Milli-Q purification system (Darmstadt, Germany).

Nicolet iS10 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, Massachusetts, USA), HT7700 transmission electron microscope (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan), VG ESCALAB 210 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, Massachusetts, USA), V-3900 UV–visible spectrometer (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan), F7000 fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan), and FLS1000 steady/transient state fluorescence spectroscopy (Edinburgh Instruments) were used. The Primaide HPLC system was equipped with a manual injector with a 20 μL sample loop, 1310 column oven, 1110 pump, and 1430 diode array detector (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan).

For the synthesis of R-CDs, a simple hydrothermal method reported in the literature (Zhao et al., 2019) was used. First, 0.2 g of o-phenylenediamine was dissolved in 1 mL of phosphoric acid and 29 mL of deionized water. The solution was then transferred to a Teflon-sealed autoclave and heated at 160 °C for 5 h. After cooling to room temperature, the red-brown solution was filtered through a 0.22-μm nylon membrane. The filtrate was placed in a dialysis bag (500–1000 Da) for three days, and the external water was changed three times a day. Finally, R-CD powders were obtained by freeze-drying.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of the BB standard solution
ce:section.ce:para: The detection process for BB is simple and fast. Specifically, 500 μL of R-CD solution (1.0 mg mL−1) was placed in a 10 mL centrifuge tube, to which different concentrations of BB standard solutions, diluted to 5.0 mL with phosphate buffer (pH = 2.0), were added. After 1 min of incubation, the solution was placed in a fluorescence spectrometer, the emission spectrum was measured after excitation at 560 nm for 5 min, and the fluorescence intensity at 627 nm was recorded. For the preparation of standard curves, the experiments for each concentration determination were repeated at least three times according to the above steps.

A commercial cocktail of Rose + Whisky flavor (RIO, China) and blueberry flavor Ramune (HATA, Japan) was obtained from the local market (RT Mart, Qingdao, China). These drinks were detected directly, without pretreatment. The detection process was the same as that used for the BB standard solution. Specifically, drinks (2.5 mL) were added to a 10 mL centrifuge tube containing 500 μL of R-CD solution (1.0 mg mL−1), diluted to 5.0 mL with phosphate buffer (pH = 2.0), and incubated for 1 min. Fluorescence spectra were measured after excitation at 560 nm for 5 min. The experiments for the determination of BB in drinks were repeated according to the above processes using three samples for each beverage.

The morphology of the R-CDs was observed by transmission electron microscopy. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the synthesized R-CDs were dispersed as spherical nanoparticles. The statistical distribution of the particle sizes was obtained by measuring the sizes of 200 particles, as shown in the diagram. The R-CDs were in the range of 2–7 nm, with an average size of 3.02 nm. The chemical composition of the R-CDs was investigated using Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. Fig. 2b shows the Fourier transform infrared spectrum of the R-CDs; the wide adsorption peak at 3362 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibrations of N–H and O–H chemical bonds. The adsorption peaks at 1770 cm−1 and 1658 cm−1 originate from the stretching vibrations of CO and CN, respectively. The adsorption peak at 1073 cm−1 was derived from the stretching vibration of C–O–C, and the peak at 864 cm−1 was due to the C–H bending vibration in the benzene ring. In addition, we observed characteristic peaks of PO and PO4 3− at 1160 cm−1 and 528 cm−1, respectively. According to Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy analysis, functional groups containing oxygen, nitrogen, and phosphorus, such as amino, hydroxyl, carbonyl, and phosphate groups, were present in the synthesized R-CDs. The X-ray photoelectron spectrum of the R-CDs (Fig. 2c) shows four peaks at 283.8 eV, 398.5 eV, 530.3 eV, and 132.3 eV, which are ascribed to C1s, N1s, O1s, and P2p, respectively. C, N, O, and P accounted for 37.3%, 6.4%, 46.6%, and 9.7% of the R-CDs, respectively. The C1s spectrum of the R-CDs (Fig. 2d) can be divided into three peaks at 283.6 eV, 285.1 eV, and 287.0 eV, which can be attributed to CC/C–C, C–N/C–O/C–P, and CO, respectively. The O1s spectrum of the R-CDs (Fig. 2e) can be split into three peaks at 530.0 eV, 531.4 eV, and 534.0 eV, indicating the presence of CO, C–O, and P–O. The N1s spectrum of the R-CDs (Fig. 2f) can be divided into two peaks that are well fitted to pyrrolic N (398.5 eV) and graphitic N (400.7 eV). The P2p spectrum of the R-CDs (Fig. 2g) revealed the presence of P–O (133.1 eV) and P–C/P–N (133.6 eV). These experimental data were consistent with previously reported results (Feng et al., 2020; Jin et al., 2020; Zhao et al., 2019). Thus, the synthesized R-CDs have abundant surface functional groups and an N- and P-doping skeleton.

The absolute fluorescence quantum yield of the as-synthesized R-CDs was 11.74%. The optical properties of the R-CDs were investigated using UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy. Fig. 2h shows the UV–vis absorption spectrum of the R-CDs; the absorption peak centered at 275 nm can be attributed to the π–π* transition of the conjugated CC structure. The absorption peaks at 570 and 612 nm are related to the n–π* transition of the surface functional groups (Pu et al., 2021). The absorption at long wavelengths is associated with the narrowing of the electron bandgap, which is always found in red fluorescent CDs (Hola et al., 2017). The fluorescence emission spectra of R-CDs at different excitation wavelengths are shown in Fig. 2i. When the excitation wavelength increased from 440 nm to 580 nm, the maximum emission wavelength remained at 627 nm, demonstrating excitation-independent emission characteristics. The fluorescence intensity was the strongest under excitation at 560 nm; thus, the maximum excitation wavelength of the R-CDs was 560 nm.

To establish an optimum detection method, conditions such as the dosage of R-CDs, medium pH, and incubation time should be optimized after testing the photoluminescence stability of the R-CDs. As shown in Fig. S1, the fluorescence intensity decreased slightly during the first 5 min of irradiation and remained essentially constant in the subsequent 60 min of irradiation. After continuous irradiation at 560 nm for 1 h, the fluorescence intensity was retained at 96% of the original intensity, showing good photobleaching resistance. The R-CD solution can be stored at 0–4 °C for more than six months, with a long shelf life. The R-CD solution emitted intense fluorescence at 627 nm when excited at 560 nm. The fluorescence was quenched immediately after the addition of BB. Fluorescence quenching reached its maximum after incubation with BB for 1 min and remained unchanged for the next 1 h (Fig. S2). Thus, BB can be detected after being fully mixed with the R-CDs. To standardize the assay procedure, the incubation time was set to 1 min. Considering the photobleaching of R-CDs, the fluorescence spectra were scanned after excitation at 560 nm for 5 min to obtain more reliable detection results.

The effect of pH on the fluorescence properties of the R-CDs was explored. When the pH of the R-CD solution was increased from 2.0 to 7.0, the fluorescence intensity at 627 nm decreased (Fig. S3). This may be due to a change in the surface charge of the R-CDs. The influence of pH on the fluorescence quenching efficiency of BB was investigated further (Fig. S4). In the 1.0 μM BB solution, the fluorescence quenching degree (F0/F) changed slightly with the change in pH. Considering the influence of pH on the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs and the fluorescence quenching efficiency of BB, the optimal pH was set to 2.0.

Finally, the use of the R-CDs for BB detection was optimized. The fluorescence quenching values (F0/F) of the solutions containing different volumes (200 μL, 500 μL, 700 μL, and 1000 μL) of the R-CDs (0.1 mg mL−1) were measured, and the results are shown in Fig. S5. The optimal quenching effect was obtained when the amount of R-CDs was 500 μL.

Under optimized conditions, the fluorescence intensity of the R-CDs decreased gradually with increasing BB concentration (Fig. 3 a). The fluorescence quenching degree exhibited a linear response to BB concentration. As shown in Fig. 3b, the linear regression equations are log(F0/F) = 0.0704 c - 0.0155 in the range of 0.2–10 μM, with R2 = 0.9994, and log(F0/F) = 0.0409 c + 0.2904 in the range of 10–40 μM, with R2 = 0.9936, where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of BB, respectively. The LOD was calculated to be 84 nM according to 3σ/k, where σ is the standard deviation, and k is the slope of the calibration plot. With a low LOD and wide linear range, this method can detect BB with good sensitivity and monitor whether the amount of BB in food exceeds the maximum limit.

In addition to sensitivity, selectivity is another key factor for evaluating the performance of probes for target detection. The effects of common food substrates, such as electrolytes and biomolecules, on the fluorescence of the system were tested. The tested interferents included Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, I−, Cl−, CO3 2−, HCO3 −, SO4 2−, glucose, fructose, sucrose, citric acid, vitamin C, vitamin B1, and vitamin B6. As shown in Fig. 3c, only BB exhibited a significant quenching effect on the fluorescence of R-CDs, while the other substrates did not cause a visible change in the fluorescence intensity, even when their concentration was 100-fold that of BB. The experimental data showed that R-CDs as fluorescent probes have high selectivity for BB and that this method is suitable for the determination of BB in complex food samples.

To verify the practicability of the developed method, one kind of cocktail (RIO) named R1 and one kind of soft drink (HATA) named R2 were chosen as real samples. The detection method used for these samples is simple and does not require sample pretreatment. For each drink, 2.5 mL of the drink was directly added to the acidic solution of R-CDs (phosphate buffer with a pH of 2.0). Thus, the drink was diluted twice for detection. Usually, drinks are acidic; therefore, the acid detection medium used in this method is suitable. As shown in Fig. 3d, no fluorescent signals were detected from the two real samples under excitation at 560 nm, indicating that this method effectively avoids the interference of background fluorescence from food substrates. The fluorescence intensity of the R-CDs was reduced to varying degrees in both beverages, indicating that BB was added to these beverages. The BB contents in the two beverages were obtained according to the fluorescence quenching values and the linear relationship. The CAC stipulates that the content of BB should not exceed 200 mg/kg in wines and 100 mg/kg in water-based flavored drinks; China stipulates that the content of BB in beverages and wines should not exceed 25 mg/kg. Using this method, the concentration of BB added in R1 was detected to be 3.08 μmol/L, which corresponds to 2.38 mg/kg; the concentration of BB added in R2 was detected to be 7.96 μmol/L, which corresponds to 6.15 mg/kg. Therefore, the amount of BB added in R1 and R2 did not exceed the maximum limit stipulated by CAC and China. The detected results were very close to those obtained by the HPLC standard method (2.98 μmol/L for R1 and 7.80 μmol/L for R2), and the chromatograms and standard curve are shown in Fig. S6.

Standard addition and recovery experiments were performed to confirm the reliability of the developed method. Different concentrations of the standard solutions (0.50, 2.50, and 5.00 μmol/L) were added to the actual samples and detected using the developed method. The recovery rates were in the range of 92–110% (Table S1), which are within the allowable range of error. The above experimental results indicate that this method is not only rapid but also accurate and possesses good practicability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of the proposed method with others
ce:section.ce:para: The proposed method was compared with other reported fluorescence analysis methods for BB detection. The maximum addition limit for BB stipulated by the CAC is 200 mg/kg, which is relatively high. This means that the method should not only have a low LOD to ensure the detection of low levels of BB but also have a wide linear range to accurately determine high contents of BB, thus effectively determining if the addition exceeds the limit. As presented in Table 1 , this method is superior to or close to the LOD and linear range of other analytical methods, confirming that the proposed strategy is sensitive and practical for the detection of BB. Note that most reported studies with lower or comparable LODs do not use green nanomaterials as fluorescent probes.

The fluorescence intensity of the R-CDs can be immediately quenched by BB. To study the fluorescence quenching mechanism, UV–vis absorption spectroscopy, fluorescence emission spectroscopy, and fluorescence decay curves were studied. As shown in Fig. 4 a, an excellent overlap can be observed between the absorption spectrum of BB and emission spectrum of the R-CDs. Thus, a possible mechanism is IFE or fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). To further clarify the mechanism, the fluorescence lifetime of the R-CDs in the absence and presence of BB was measured. The energy transfer efficiency (E) of FRET can be calculated using the equation E = 1 – (τDA/τD), where τD and τDA are the donor excited state lifetimes in the absence and presence of the acceptor, respectively (Elangovan et al., 2002). The fluorescence decay curve of the R-CDs showed no significant change in the presence of BB, as shown in Fig. 4b; the measured fluorescent lifetime of the R-CDs was 1.66 ns, while the lifetime of the mixture (R-CDs + BB) was 1.55 ns, confirming that it is a static quenching mechanism (Sekar et al., 2021) and that no available FRET process occurred (Cao et al., 2014; Zhang et al., 2012). The UV–vis absorption spectra of R-CDs with and without BB addition were also investigated. We see that the adsorption of the mixture is a simple stack of the absorption bands of R-CDs and BB (Fig. 4c), and no observable variation in the absorption band was observed, which further demonstrates that no complexes were formed (Sekar et al., 2021) and that FRET between BB and R-CDs did not occur (Fang et al., 2016; Sivasankaran et al., 2017). A zeta-potential analysis of R-CDs and BB in the detection medium of phosphate buffer (pH = 2.0) was performed. Since both R-CDs and BB are positively charged (5.5 mV and 7.2 mV, respectively), the electrostatic repulsion force will prevent BB from adsorbing onto the surface of R-CDs and decrease the energy transfer efficiency of FRET to some extent. Therefore, these results demonstrate that the fluorescence quenching is mostly ascribed to IFE.

To avoid the interference of blue background fluorescence from complex food substrates, R-CDs were synthesized as fluorescent probes to detect BB in food. The R-CDs have good photoluminescence stability; the maximum emission wavelength was constant at 627 nm when the excitation wavelength changed from 480 nm to 580 nm, with a maximum excitation wavelength of 560 nm, which adequately presents the red-emitting characteristic. Common substrates in foods have no fluorescent signal in the red region and have no visible effect on the fluorescence of the R-CDs. BB shows a linear quenching effect on the R-CDs in the range of 0.2–40 μM with an LOD of 84.00 nM. The fluorescence quenching mechanism can be explained by IFE. This method can accurately detect BB in cocktails and beverages without sample pretreatment. This study reveals that R-CDs, as low-cost, green, and excellent fluorescent nanomaterials, have great potential in food analysis.

This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (No. ZR2020QB087), the High-Level Talents Scientific Research Foundation of Qingdao Agricultural University (No. 6651120016).

Qi Wu: Conceptualization, Writing – original draft, Data curation, Formal analysis, Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Xin-Jiao Duan: Methodology, Investigation. Hai-Tao Lv: Validation. Li-Tao Wang: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodcont.2023.109719.
